message.content:
You are researching how the BEST deep-research agents beat the human reference on DeepResearch Bench (RACE).

POLARIS's MEASURED position on DeepResearch Bench task 72 (RACE, gpt-5.5 evaluator, k=5 pinned):
  overall 0.4382 | comprehensiveness 0.4549 | insight 0.4238 | instruction-following 0.4409 | readability 0.3774
  baseline was 0.4062. Human reference = 0.5000 by construction. SOTA (ADORE) = 0.5265. Our gap = 0.088.
  RACE formula: Overall = target/(target+reference), an LLM judge scores BOTH against per-task weighted criteria.
  Task-72 dimension weights: insight 0.32, comprehensiveness 0.29, instruction-following 0.25, readability 0.14.
  KEY MECHANIC WE VERIFIED: RACE runs an LLM "ArticleCleaner" that STRIPS ALL CITATION MARKERS, REFERENCE LISTS
  AND FOOTNOTES before the judge reads anything (our 9,300 words -> 7,692; 345 [n] markers -> 0; bibliography deleted).
  So citation/bibliography quality is INVISIBLE to RACE. Only the running prose scores.
  OUR KNOWN WEAKNESS: the judge reads 12 paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets.
  The human reference: 59 paragraphs averaging 142 words, 24 H3, 10 tables, 91 bullets. We LIST findings; the
  reference EXPLAINS them. Our pipeline span-grounds every sentence, which structurally forbids cross-source
  inference — the sentence type that earns INSIGHT (0.32, heaviest weight, our worst dimension).

YOUR ANGLE: How the RACE judge actually assigns points — read the scoring prompt in github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench (prompt/score_prompt_en.py, deepresearch_bench_race.py) and the DeepResearch Bench paper (arXiv 2506.11763). What EXACTLY does the judge reward on INSIGHT and COMPREHENSIVENESS? Is the score reference-RELATIVE (target/(target+reference))? What makes a report beat a human reference — i.e. what did >0.50 systems do that the human did not?

Use WebSearch and WebFetch aggressively — multiple queries, follow the links, READ the primary sources (papers, repos, docs), do not stop at a search snippet. Prefer papers/code/official docs over blog summaries. If you cannot verify something, mark it confidence=low and say so rather than guessing. We have already been burned tonight by a plausible-but-stale number (we planned against 48.88, which turned out to be a year out of date).

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DeepResearch Bench RACE framework:

## Overview

DeepResearch Bench is a benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.

## RACE Framework

RACE is a Reference-based and Adaptive Criteria-driven Evaluation framework with Dynamic Weighting, which targets the assessment of report generation quality. The framework uses a reference-based method with adaptive criteria to assess the quality of generated research reports.

## Reference-Based Scoring

The reference reports used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025.

## Evaluation Dimensions

The RACE framework uses the LLM-as-a-Judge method, using Gemini-2.5-pro to assess report quality based on four dimensions: Comprehensiveness, Insight/Depth, Instruction-Following, and Readability. The framework operates in three key stages: dynamic weight and adaptive criteria generation, reference-based scoring, and overall score calculation. By comparing target reports with high-quality references, RACE mitigates biases and ensures a more accurate evaluation of report quality.

The paper (arXiv 2506.11763) has been made openly available, with DeepResearch Bench and key components of these frameworks open-sourced at https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Summary of Evaluation Prompts

This file contains four distinct prompt templates for evaluating research articles:

## 1. **Merged Evaluation Prompt** (`generate_merged_score_prompt`)
Compares two articles across four dimensions by dynamically generating evaluation criteria. Requires task description, both articles, and custom criteria list as inputs.

## 2. **Static Evaluation Prompt** (`generate_static_score_prompt`)
Compares two articles using 16 fixed criteria organized into four categories:
- **Comprehensiveness** (4 criteria): coverage breadth, depth, factual support, multiple perspectives
- **Insight** (4 criteria): originality, logic, problem-solving, forward-thinking
- **Instruction Following** (3 criteria): task response, scope adherence, requirement coverage
- **Readability** (4 criteria): structure, language, terminology, presentation

Each criterion includes a weight value (ranging 0.25-0.34).

## 3. **Point-Wise Scoring Prompt** (`point_wise_score_prompt`)
Evaluates a single article against dynamic criteria without comparative reference, producing individual scores per criterion rather than comparative analysis.

## 4. **Vanilla Prompt**
Requests a single overall score (0-10) with brief justification for one article against a task, without detailed criterion-by-criterion breakdown.

**Scoring Scale (All Prompts):** 0-2 (very poor), 2-4 (poor), 4-6 (average), 6-8 (good), 8-10 (excellent)

import logging

def calculate_weighted_scores(llm_output_json, criteria_data, language="en"):
    """
    Calculates weighted scores based on LLM output and criteria weights.
    
    Args:
        llm_output_json: JSON output from LLM with scoring data
        criteria_data: Criteria configuration with dimension weights
        language: Language of the evaluation (default: "en")
        
    Returns:
        Dictionary with weighted scores for target and reference models
    """
    results = {
        "target": {"dims": {}, "total": 0.0},
        "reference": {"dims": {}, "total": 0.0}
    }
    total_target_score = 0.0
    total_reference_score = 0.0
    
    # Get dimension weights
    dimension_weights = criteria_data.get("dimension_weight", {})
    task_id = criteria_data.get("id", "Unknown") 
    
    # Check if criterions exist
    if "criterions" not in criteria_data or not criteria_data["criterions"]:
        error_msg = f"ID: {task_id} - Missing required criterions data, cannot calculate weighted scores"
        logging.error(error_msg)
        raise ValueError(error_msg)
    
    # Create a mapping from criterion text to weight for easier lookup
    criterion_weights = {}
    for dim, criterions in criteria_data.get("criterions", {}).items():
        criterion_weights[dim] = {crit['criterion']: crit['weight'] for crit in criterions}

    # Record all unmatched criteria for warnings
    unmatched_criteria = set()
    
    for dim, scores_list in llm_output_json.items():
        if not isinstance(scores_list, list):
            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' in LLM output is not a list. Skipping.")
            continue

        if dim not in dimension_weights:
            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' from LLM output not found in criteria dimension weights. Skipping dimension.")
            continue
            
        if dim not in criterion_weights:
            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' from LLM output not found in criteria details. Skipping dimension.")
            continue

        dim_target_weighted_sum = 0.0
        dim_reference_weighted_sum = 0.0
        dim_total_weight = 0.0

        dim_criteria_map = criterion_weights.get(dim, {})
        
        # Skip dimension if no criteria mapping exists
        if not dim_criteria_map:
            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - No criteria mapping found for dimension '{dim}'. Skipping dimension.")
            continue

        for score_item in scores_list:
            if not isinstance(score_item, dict):
                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Item in scores_list for dimension '{dim}' is not a dictionary. Skipping item: {score_item}")
                continue

            criterion_text_raw = score_item.get("criterion")
            criterion_text = criterion_text_raw.strip() if isinstance(criterion_text_raw, str) else None

            # Check different score field formats
            # 1. Comparative scoring mode: article_1_score and article_2_score
            # 2. Single scoring mode: target_score
            article_1_score_raw = score_item.get("article_1_score")
            article_2_score_raw = score_item.get("article_2_score")
            target_score_raw = score_item.get("target_score")  # Single scoring mode

            # If target_score exists but article_1_score doesn't, assign target_score to article_1_score
            if target_score_raw is not None and article_1_score_raw is None:
                article_1_score_raw = target_score_raw
                # In single scoring mode, article_2_score is not needed

            try:
                article_1_score = float(article_1_score_raw) if article_1_score_raw is not None else None
                article_2_score = float(article_2_score_raw) if article_2_score_raw is not None else None
            except (ValueError, TypeError):
                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Invalid score format for criterion '{criterion_text}' in dimension '{dim}'. Scores: art1='{article_1_score_raw}', art2='{article_2_score_raw}'. Skipping criterion.")
                continue

            # If criterion_text exists and article_1_score is not None
            if criterion_text and article_1_score is not None:
                # Check for exact match
                weight = dim_criteria_map.get(criterion_text)
                
                # If exact match not found, try fuzzy matching
                if weight is None:
                    # First try case-insensitive matching
                    criterion_lower = criterion_text.lower()
                    for key, val in dim_criteria_map.items():
                        if key.lower() == criterion_lower:
                            weight = val
                            break
                    
                    # If still not found, try substring matching
                    if weight is None:
                        for key, val in dim_criteria_map.items():
                            # Check if criterion text contains criteria or criteria contains criterion text
                            if criterion_lower in key.lower() or key.lower() in criterion_lower:
                                weight = val
                                break
                
                # If still no match found, record and use average weight
                if weight is None:
                    unmatched_criteria.add(f"{dim}:{criterion_text}")
                    # Calculate average weight for this dimension's criteria
                    weight = sum(dim_criteria_map.values()) / len(dim_criteria_map)
                    
                dim_target_weighted_sum += article_1_score * weight
                dim_total_weight += weight
                
                # Only calculate reference part if article_2_score exists
                if article_2_score is not None:
                    dim_reference_weighted_sum += article_2_score * weight
            else:
                if criterion_text:
                    if dim not in getattr(calculate_weighted_scores, '_warned_dims', set()):
                        logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Criterion text mismatch for dimension '{dim}': '{criterion_text}'. Available criteria keys start with: {list(dim_criteria_map.keys())[:1] if dim_criteria_map else []}... Check prompt/output/criteria file consistency. Further mismatches in this dim won't be logged fully.")
                        if not hasattr(calculate_weighted_scores, '_warned_dims'): calculate_weighted_scores._warned_dims = set()
                        calculate_weighted_scores._warned_dims.add(dim)
                    else:
                        logging.debug(f"ID: {task_id} - Another criterion mismatch for dimension '{dim}': '{criterion_text}'")
                elif not criterion_text:
                    logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Missing 'criterion' key in score item for dimension '{dim}': {score_item}. Skipping item.")

        if dim_total_weight > 0:
            dim_target_avg = dim_target_weighted_sum / dim_total_weight
            # Only calculate reference average if reference scores exist
            dim_reference_avg = dim_reference_weighted_sum / dim_total_weight if article_2_score is not None else 0
        else:
            dim_target_avg = 0
            dim_reference_avg = 0
            if len(scores_list) > 0:
                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - No valid criteria scored for dimension '{dim}' despite {len(scores_list)} items in LLM output. Check for systematic mismatches. Dimension average set to 0.")

        results["target"]["dims"][f"{dim}_weighted_avg"] = dim_target_avg
        results["reference"]["dims"][f"{dim}_weighted_avg"] = dim_reference_avg

        dim_weight = dimension_weights.get(dim, 0)
        total_target_score += dim_target_avg * dim_weight
        total_reference_score += dim_reference_avg * dim_weight

    # Log warning if there are unmatched criteria
    if unmatched_criteria:
        logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - {len(unmatched_criteria)} criteria without exact matches: {unmatched_criteria}")
    
    results["target"]["total"] = total_target_score
    results["reference"]["total"] = total_reference_score

    return results

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard rankings for the RACE evaluation:

## Top Performers on DeepResearch Bench RACE Leaderboard

The top-ranked systems include: 1) Onyx Deep Research, 2) WhaleCloud DocChain, 3) Cellcog (with an overall score of 54.54), 4) Qianfan-DeepResearch Pro (54.22), and 5) Qianfan-DeepResearch (53.07).

# AgentCPM-Report: Key Findings

## RACE/DeepResearch Bench Scores

AgentCPM-Report achieves:
- **Comprehensiveness: 50.54** (vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro: 49.71)
- **Insight: 52.64** (vs. Gemini-2.5-Pro: 49.45)
- **Overall: 50.11** (surpassing Gemini-2.5-Pro's 49.71)

## Techniques Improving Insight & Comprehensiveness

The system employs **WARP (Writing As Reasoning Policy)**, which alternates between:

1. **Evidence-Based Drafting**: Retrieval queries conditioned on accumulating narrative to ensure logical consistency
2. **Reasoning-Driven Deepening**: The model detects logical gaps in drafts and autonomously expands underdeveloped sections

The authors state this approach enables models to "detect logical gaps or superficial arguments that were invisible during initial planning" by treating "the newly generated draft as a fresh observation for reasoning."

## Ablation Evidence for Insight Gains

Table 3 directly compares paradigms. When using the same large model (Qwen3-235B), WARP outperforms plan-then-write across metrics, with Insight improving by +1.19 points. This demonstrates that "draft-aware deepening is a key driver of insight."

## Report Structure & Synthesis

The paper notes the system produces reports with hierarchical depth (three levels maximum), and training drives agents to increase expansion actions from 4.44 to 8.63 steps, generating "substantially richer structure" with more granular subsectioning.

Regarding presentation limitations, they acknowledge tables require "reasoning fundamentally different from prose," suggesting this remains a bottleneck for smaller models.

import re
def stats(f,name):
    t=open(f).read()
    # strip code fences
    words=len(re.findall(r"\S+",t))
    lines=t.split("\n")
    h1=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r"^# ",l)])
    h2=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r"^## ",l)])
    h3=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r"^### ",l)])
    h4=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r"^#### ",l)])
    bullets=len([l for l in lines if re.match(r"^\s*([-*+]|\d+\.)\s",l)])
    tbl_rows=len([l for l in lines if l.strip().startswith("|")])
    tables=len(re.findall(r"\n\s*\|[^\n]*\|\s*\n\s*\|[\s:|-]+\|",t))
    paras=[p for p in re.split(r"\n\s*\n",t) if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith(("#","|","-","*",">")) and not re.match(r"^\d+\.",p.strip())]
    avg=sum(len(p.split()) for p in paras)/max(len(paras),1)
    print(f"--- {name} ---")
    print(f"words={words}  H1={h1} H2={h2} H3={h3} H4={h4}  bullets={bullets}  tables={tables} (table rows={tbl_rows})")
    print(f"body paragraphs={len(paras)}  avg words/para={avg:.0f}")
for f,n in [("q72.md","QIANFAN #1 (58.03) task 72"),("o72.md","ONYX (54.54) task 72")]:

EOF

Prioritize top journals (AER, QJE, JPE, Econometrica, REStud, JEP; plus Management Science, MISQ, SMJ) and clearly state identification strategies and main quantitative findings.
  #5: Research peer-reviewed English-language journal evidence on AI-driven labor-market restructuring across specific industries: manufacturing, finance, healthcare, transportation/logistics, retail, professional services, media/creative, and software/IT. Use only journal articles; exclude working papers and reports.
  #6: Research peer-reviewed English-language journal literature on institutional/policy/organizational moderators of AI’s labor impacts: labor-market regulation, unions/collective bargaining, social protection and ALMPs, training investments, competition/market power and superstar firms, and governance. Identify key unresolved debates and research gaps (measurement, causality, generative AI evidence, cross-country comparability), using journal sources only.

============================================================

ANSWER:
### 1) Positioning AI as a Fourth Industrial Revolution (4IR) restructuring force

Across high-quality journal literatures in economics, management, and information systems, *AI* is treated less as a single “technology” than as a **bundle of capabilities**—notably machine learning–based prediction, classification, and language/vision processing—that can be embedded into software, platforms, decision systems, and (in some settings) robotics. This embedding is crucial for labor-market restructuring: AI’s effects often operate through **repartitioning tasks** within jobs, **reorganizing workflows** within establishments, and **re-sorting activities across firms and industries**, rather than simply “eliminating occupations.”

A central insight that runs through the best journal evidence is that AI-driven disruption is **simultaneously**:
- **Substitutive** for some tasks (automation/displacement),
- **Complementary** to other tasks (augmentation/productivity),
- **Recompositional**, changing the task mix and skill content of jobs, and
- **Reallocative**, shifting work across firms, places, and industries.

This multi-channel view aligns closely with the 4IR framing: AI interacts with digitalization, data infrastructure, platforms, and (sometimes) robots, producing restructuring that is uneven across sectors, occupations, and worker groups.

content.content:
• Workflow rigidity. Treating retrieval as a single pre-processing
step limits recovery from early mistakes. Static “chains” of-
ten lack the agency to adjust strategy when intermediate
evidence indicates a mismatch or missing dimension [14].
• Contextual myopia. Long-context ingestion does not
guarantee utilization; important details can be ignored even
when retrieved [11]. Moreover, one-pass synthesis lacks
self-correction loops that can systematically target missing
or weakly supported sections [2, 12].
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Positioning vs. prior deep-research agents. ADORE is inspired by
recent deep-research systems, but it differs in what drives iteration
and what constitutes convergence. WebWeaver emphasizes outline-
centric evidence structuring and section-level writing with a mem-
ory bank [10]. TTD-DR frames long-form generation as test-time
diffusion over drafts, improving quality via iterative denoising steps
[7]. In contrast, ADORE introduces Memory-locked synthesis: we
treat the Memory Bank as a hard constraint on generation (section-
scoped admissible evidence with explicit claim–evidence linkage),
then use section-level evidence coverage audits to diagnose gaps
and drive targeted follow-up retrieval with an evidence-driven
stopping rule. This mechanism is operationalized via Evidence-
coverage–guided execution, with an explicit evidence-coverage
stopping rule rather than a fixed iteration budget or outline-only
convergence.
ADORE overview. ADORE (Adaptive Deep Orchestration for Re-
search in Enterprise) replaces linear “retrieve-then-write” with an
agentic workflow that mirrors human research: scoping via clarifica-
tion, plan formation, iterative evidence collection, and verification.
The core design choice is a structured Memory Bank that stores
evidence with explicit claim–evidence linkage, enabling traceable
report generation and systematic checks for coverage and citation
stability.
Contributions. We make the following contributions (using con-
sistent terminology throughout the paper):
(1) Memory-locked synthesis. We generate long-form re-
ports constrained to a structured Memory Bank (Claim–Evidence
Graph with section-level admissible evidence), ensuring
that claims are traceable and citations are grounded in re-
trieved sources.
(2) Evidence-coverage–guided execution. We use section-
level evidence coverage audits to localize weak or missing
sections, trigger targeted follow-up retrieval, and terminate
via an evidence-driven stopping criterion [2, 13].
(3) Section-packed long-context grounding. We make long-
form grounded reporting tractable by packing section-specific
evidence, pruning redundant context, and compressing re-
trieved content into citation-preserving summaries so syn-
thesis stays within context limits [3, 11].
(4) We implement an adaptive orchestrator that routes between
fast retrieval and deep multi-agent investigation, and we
demonstrate state-of-the-art results on both public and in-
ternal benchmarks [4, 5].
You et al.

content.text: I have everything I need. Two of the parent's premises are wrong, and I found the top systems' actual task-72 reports.

findings.claim: PREMISE BUSTED: the RACE reference is NOT human-written. It is Gemini-2.5-pro Deep Research output from April 2025. 'Human reference = 0.5000' is false.
findings.detail: Direct quote from the paper (Experimental Setup): 'The reference reports (Rref) used in RACE's scoring methodology were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research, as available in April 2025.' Corroborated independently by the leaderboards: gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch scores 49.98 (gpt-5.5 judge) and 49.71 (original judge) — i.e. ~0.50, because it is being scored against a sibling sample of its own output. CONSEQUENCE FOR POLARIS: the thing we must beat is a known, imitable, year-old LLM's house style — not a human. Its 59 paragraphs / 142 words-per-para / 24 H3 / 10 tables / 91 bullets is *Gemini DR's* template, and it has known weaknesses (it is the weakest-insight report among all >50 systems). The '0.5000 by construction' intuition is still right (reference vs itself = 0.5), but the 'human' part is wrong and it has been leading us to over-respect the reference.

findings.claim: PREMISE BUSTED (stale number, again): ADORE at 0.5265 is NOT SOTA. Current SOTA on the gpt-5.5 evaluator we are pinned to is cellcog-max at 0.5578. Our real gap is 0.1196, not 0.088.
findings.detail: I pulled the live leaderboard CSVs directly. The HF Space has TWO leaderboards, and one is literally named for our judge. data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (gpt-5.5 evaluator, the one POLARIS is pinned to): cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-DR 49.98, openai-DR 47.84, perplexity 43.05, grok 41.22. data/leaderboard.csv (original judge): qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06 ... tavily-research 52.44. ADORE appears on NEITHER. ADORE=52.65 is real (arXiv 2601.18267, 'Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG') and the paper does claim 'rank #1 on the leaderboard', but its Table 2 baselines are LangChain(Tavily)/OpenAI-DR — an old snapshot. It is now mid-pack. NOTE: POLARIS 0.4382 currently sits between perplexity (43.05) and openai-DR (47.84) on our judge. WORSE: that same ADORE paper is almost certainly the source of our 'human reference' error — it states 'the benchmark is calibrated such that a score of 50.0 represents the human-written reference', which contradicts the primary DRB paper.

findings.claim: The overall score is a ratio of WEIGHTED TOTALS, not a weighted average of the four published dimension scores. Marginal gain per absolute criterion point is proportional to the dimension weight, making INSIGHT (0.32 on task 72) the single highest-leverage point to win.
findings.detail: From score_calculator.py + deepresearch_bench_race.py. Per dimension d: S_d(R) = (sum_k w_dk * s_dk) / (sum_k w_dk)  [criterion weights, renormalized]. Then S_int(R) = sum_d W_d * S_d(R)  [dimension weights]. Then overall = S_int(tgt) / (S_int(tgt) + S_int(ref)). The four *reported* dimension numbers are computed separately as S_d(tgt)/(S_d(tgt)+S_d(ref)) — so the published overall is NOT the weighted mean of the published dimensions (ratio-of-sums != sum-of-ratios). Differentiating: d(overall)/dS_d(tgt) = W_d * S_int(ref) / (S_int(tgt)+S_int(ref))^2 — i.e. the payoff for +1 absolute point is exactly proportional to W_d, independent of how the reference scores on that dimension. So on task 72 an absolute point of insight (0.32) is worth 2.3x an absolute point of readability (0.14). ARITHMETIC ON OUR POSITION: overall 0.4382 => S_int(tgt)/S_int(ref) = 0.780. To merely TIE the reference (0.50) we need +28% absolute weighted score. To reach cellcog-max (0.5578) we need +62%. This is a much bigger lift than the 0.088 framing implied.

findings.claim: There is NO position randomization: the target report is ALWAYS article_1 and the reference is ALWAYS article_2, on every single judge call.
findings.detail: deepresearch_bench_race.py line ~99: merged_score_prompt.format(task_prompt=..., article_1=target_article, article_2=reference_article, criteria_list=...). grep for shuffle|random|swap|position over the whole file returns nothing. So the judge sees us first and the Gemini reference second, always. Two consequences: (a) whatever positional bias the judge has is a CONSTANT, not noise — it does not average out over k=5 or over tasks, and it is already baked into every leaderboard number, so we should not try to 'correct' for it; (b) the judge is doing an explicitly COMPARATIVE read (the prompt says 'deeply compare two articles', 'Comparative Evaluation', and emits an 'analysis' field contrasting them before scoring). Scores are not independent absolute judgments. What matters is being *visibly, contrastively* better on each named criterion — a report that is good in isolation but offers nothing the reference lacks will land at ~0.5.

findings.claim: The INSIGHT criteria are auto-generated per task with an explicit instruction to reward reasoning ACROSS sources and to punish exactly what POLARIS does (span-grounded listing).
findings.detail: criteria_prompt_en.py, generate_eval_criteria_prompt_insight. Core Requirement #2 verbatim: 'Beyond Surface-Level: Criteria should assess analytical depth, logical rigor, originality of insights, and value of conclusions, NOT JUST INFORMATION LISTING.' The few-shot example criteria it is told to imitate: 'Depth of Analysis of Core Drivers and Mechanisms' — 'deeply analyzes their interplay and causal mechanisms, rather than a superficial listing'; 'Sophistication in Uncovering Market Impacts' — 'goes beyond obvious impacts to uncover subtle or SECOND-ORDER effects'; 'Logical Coherence and Justification of Strategies'; 'Originality and Strategic Value of Insights' — 'challenges conventional wisdom'; 'Future Outlook and Adaptability'. Every one of these is satisfiable ONLY by a sentence whose truth-maker is the relation between multiple sources, not any single span. Our per-sentence span-grounding invariant makes these criteria structurally unscoreable for us — this is the direct mechanical cause of insight being our worst dimension at the heaviest weight. The static-criteria fallback (score_prompt_en.py) names the same four: Analysis Depth and Originality / Logical Reasoning and Causal Relationships / Problem Insight and Solutions / Forward-Looking and Inspirational Thinking.

findings.claim: LENGTH IS NOT THE LEVER. Onyx beat the reference on task 72 (0.5626) with a 4,554-word report — shorter than ours. Its edge is paragraph granularity and structure, not volume.
findings.detail: Onyx published per-task raw RACE results AND its actual reports. Its task-72 numbers (from eval_results/run_1/raw_results.jsonl, id=72): overall 0.5626, comprehensiveness 0.5662, insight 0.5813, instruction_following 0.5599, readability 0.5213 — vs POLARIS 0.4382/0.4549/0.4238/0.4409/0.3774. I measured its report_logs/72.md: ANSWER body = 4,554 words, 11 H3, 25 H4, 79 bullets, 0 tables, 24 body paragraphs averaging 49 WORDS. POLARIS: 7,692 words, 12 paragraphs averaging 633 words, 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets. Meanwhile the #1 system overall (qianfan, 58.03) went the opposite way on length — 71,504 words on task 72 — yet lands in the same place. The variable the two winners SHARE, and we lack, is not length: it is paragraph granularity (49 and 130 words/para vs our 633) and heading density. Our 633-word monoliths give the judge no addressable unit to map onto any named criterion.

findings.claim: The insight-manufacturing device in the #1 report is a NAMED SECTION per chapter whose entire job is cross-source adjudication — 'Synthesis', 'Sources of Divergence', 'Reconciling', 'Comparative Assessment'.
findings.detail: Qianfan (rank 1, 58.03) published its literal task-72 report. Structure: 11 H1 chapters, 61 H2, 25 H3, 13 tables, 492 paragraphs at ~130 words. Every chapter TERMINATES in an adjudication section: '2.7 Comparative Assessment: Convergences, Divergences, and Implications'; '3.4 Sources of Divergence: Measurement, Horizon, Geography, and Identification'; '3.5 Synthesis: What the Aggregate Evidence Does and Does Not Establish'; '4.6 Methodological Reconciliation: Occupation-Level versus Task-Level Exposure'; '5.1 Reconciling the divergent trajectories'; '8.7 Synthesis: Organizational Implementation as the Decisive Intermediary'. The PROSE inside them explicitly labels epistemic status across the corpus: 'Bringing the four preceding sections together, the aggregate-employment evidence supports a set of carefully calibrated claims while leaving several first-order questions genuinely open. Three propositions enjoy reasonably strong **emerging consensus** across the methodologically diverse literature. First, **meaningful local displacement effects are well-documented**... Second, **offsetting product-demand and complementarity channels operate with quantitatively first-order magnitudes**...' Note the bolded claim-topic sentences. ACTIONABLE FOR POLARIS: this is a structural, not stylistic, fix — allocate explicit sections that are LICENSED to contain inference-over-sources (consensus / divergence / reconciliation / what-the-evidence-does-not-establish) and exempt them from the per-sentence span-grounding invariant, grounding them instead in the SET of spans they adjudicate. Confidence medium because this is observed correlation in the top reports, not an ablation.

findings.claim: Every system that beats the reference is INSIGHT-LED; every system that loses to it is READABILITY-LED. Insight is where the Gemini reference is weakest and headroom is largest; readability is the hardest dimension to beat it on.
findings.detail: Computed insight-minus-readability from the gpt-5.5 leaderboard: cellcog-max +5.14 (57.08 vs 51.94), lunon +4.35, WhaleCloud +2.85, bodhi +2.73, sourcery +2.54, gemini-ref +0.34, openai-DR -0.93, perplexity -4.76, grok -7.60. The sign flips exactly at the 50-line. Same on the original leaderboard, more extremely: qianfan insight 61.48 vs readability 54.34; zhipu 60.14 vs 53.88. And Onyx's 10-run means: insight 0.5683 (highest dim) vs readability 0.5239 (lowest dim). INTERPRETATION: no top system beats the Gemini reference by much on readability — they all top out around 52 — because Gemini DR's formatting is genuinely excellent. Insight is the soft spot: winners clear it by 5-11 points. This says the ceiling on 'make it prettier' is ~+2 over reference, whereas the ceiling on 'make it reason across sources' is ~+11. Combined with the weight (0.32 vs 0.14), insight is where essentially all of the available score is.

findings.claim: POLARIS's readability (0.3774) is below every system on the leaderboard by 8+ points — worse than grok. This is not a polish problem; it is the missing scoring surface.
findings.detail: Worst readability on the gpt-5.5 board is grok-deeper-search at 45.72; every other system is 46-53. POLARIS is 37.74. We are 8 points below the floor of the entire field. The readability criteria (score_prompt_en.py static set, and the task-specific generated equivalents) include 'Clear Structure and Logic' (introduction/body/conclusion, coherent paragraph organization) and 'Information Presentation and Visual Elements' ('effectively uses formatting, headings, lists, emphasis, etc. ... and appropriately uses charts'). With 0 subsections, 0 tables, 0 bullets and 633-word paragraphs we score near-zero on two of four readability criteria. IMPORTANT SECOND-ORDER EFFECT: the same intervention pays into the other dimensions — tables score under comprehensiveness's 'Data and Factual Support', and named synthesis headings create the addressable surface the judge maps insight criteria onto. Readability's own weight is only 0.14, but the fix is shared infrastructure, so do it for the insight/comprehensiveness spillover, not for the 0.14.

findings.claim: The ArticleCleaner preserves ALL structure — headings, tables, bullets, bold all survive. Only citations/references/footnotes are destroyed. So structural investment is safe; citation investment is wasted.
findings.detail: clean_prompt.py (clean_article_prompt_en) verbatim: 'Cleaning goal: remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2], 1, 2, etc. or other complex citation formats), reference lists, footnotes, and ensure the content reads smoothly. KEEP ALL OTHER ORIGINAL CONTENT; remove only citations. If a citation mark wraps content that is part of a sentence, keep the text inside and drop only the marks. Special case: if the entire input is a reference / bibliography / footnote-definition section ... return an empty string.' It runs CHUNKED ('may be a complete research article or just a continuous fragment'). This confirms POLARIS's own measurement (9,300 -> 7,692 words, 345 markers -> 0) and adds the key positive: markdown headings, tables, lists and emphasis are explicitly out of scope for removal, so every structural token we add survives to the judge. Corollary: the DRB paper's own stated rationale is that 'overly lengthy or complex citation styles can adversely affect the Judge LLM's scoring' — i.e. citations were removed because they HURT scores, not because they were neutral.

findings.claim: RACE judge noise is tiny — Onyx's 10 independent eval runs give overall sigma = 0.00076 on the 100-task mean, implying ~0.008 per single task. POLARIS's 0.4062 -> 0.4382 (+0.032) is real signal, not noise.
findings.detail: Onyx published RACE EVAL AGGREGATE STATS over 10 runs: Overall mean 0.5481, SD 0.000764, min 0.5468, max 0.5492. Per-dimension SDs: comprehensiveness 0.000957, insight 0.001492, IF 0.001087, readability 0.001625. Back-solving to a single task (sigma_task ~ sigma_mean * sqrt(100)) gives ~0.0076 per task per run; at our k=5 that is ~0.0034. Our +0.032 delta is therefore roughly 9 sigma — trustworthy. Confidence is medium only on the interpretation: the repo has ONE set of report_logs and ten eval_results/run_N dirs, so these are almost certainly ten re-runs of the JUDGE over fixed reports (judge/eval variance only), not ten re-runs of the agent. That is exactly the quantity we want for k=5 pinning, but I could not find an explicit statement confirming it, so treat 'this is judge-only variance' as inferred.

findings.claim: Onyx's insight device in prose is the explicit cross-corpus meta-claim plus a bolded mechanism taxonomy — a sentence type that CANNOT be span-grounded to one source.
findings.detail: Verbatim from Onyx's task-72 answer (which scored insight 0.5813): 'A central insight that runs through the best journal evidence is that AI-driven disruption is **simultaneously**: - **Substitutive** for some tasks (automation/displacement), - **Complementary** to other tasks (augmentation/productivity), - **Recompositional**, changing the task mix and skill content of jobs, and - **Reallocative**, shifting work across firms, places, and industries.' And: 'Across high-quality journal literatures ... AI is treated less as a single "technology" than as a **bundle of capabilities**.' Note the shape: (1) a quantifier over the CORPUS ('runs through the best journal evidence', 'Across high-quality journal literatures'), (2) an original organizing taxonomy that appears in no single source, (3) bold on the analytic terms so the judge can find them, (4) delivered as a short bulleted list, not a 600-word block. This is cheap to implement and is the highest-density-per-word insight construction I found. Confidence medium: observed in a high-scoring report, not ablated.

findings.claim: An ablation exists showing draft-then-deepen (writing as reasoning) beats plan-then-write specifically on INSIGHT (+1.19), by letting the model detect logical gaps that were invisible at planning time.
findings.detail: AgentCPM-Report ('Interleaving Drafting and Deepening for Open-Ended Deep Research'). Its WARP policy alternates evidence-based drafting with reasoning-driven deepening: it treats the newly generated draft as a fresh observation, detects 'logical gaps or superficial arguments that were invisible during initial planning', and autonomously expands underdeveloped sections. Table 3, same base model (Qwen3-235B), WARP vs plan-then-write: Insight +1.19. Training drove expansion actions from 4.44 to 8.63 steps, producing 'substantially richer structure' with more granular subsectioning. Reported RACE: overall 50.11, comprehensiveness 50.54, insight 52.64 — beating the Gemini reference. They also concede tables remain a bottleneck ('require reasoning fundamentally different from prose'). RELEVANCE: this is the one published ablation I found that isolates an insight-raising mechanism, and it says the mechanism is a SECOND PASS OVER YOUR OWN DRAFT, not more retrieval. POLARIS's pipeline is single-pass span-grounded composition, which has no such stage.

findings.claim: ADORE's actual mechanism is evidence-grounding/traceability oriented (Memory Bank as a hard constraint on generation) — i.e. it is the SAME family as POLARIS's span-grounding, and it plateaus at 52.65, below the current field.
findings.detail: ADORE (Adaptive Deep Orchestration for Research in Enterprise), 4-page paper. Its three named contributions: (1) 'Memory-locked synthesis. We generate long-form reports CONSTRAINED to a structured Memory Bank (Claim-Evidence Graph with section-level admissible evidence), ensuring that claims are traceable and citations are grounded in retrieved sources.' (2) 'Evidence-coverage-guided execution' — section-level coverage audits trigger targeted follow-up retrieval, with an evidence-driven stopping rule. (3) 'Section-packed long-context grounding' — pack section-specific evidence, prune, compress into citation-preserving summaries. Reported: overall 52.65, comprehensiveness 52.22, insight 54.37, IF 51.11, readability 52.18. THE LESSON FOR US IS THE INVERSE OF WHAT THE PARENT ASSUMED: ADORE is the strongest published example of the strategy POLARIS is already running (hard evidence-locking of every claim), it reaches 52.65, and it is now beaten by ~6 points by systems on the current board. Its own insight (54.37) is its best dimension only because it also does section-level gap audits. Also note the irony: ADORE's citation-traceability machinery is aimed at a property (citation grounding) that RACE's ArticleCleaner deletes before scoring. Doubling down on grounding is a capped strategy.

findings.claim: The score_calculator silently falls back to fuzzy/substring matching and then to an AVERAGE weight when the judge's returned criterion string doesn't match the generated criteria — so criterion-text drift degrades weighting but never zeroes a dimension.
findings.detail: Match order in calculate_weighted_scores: exact criterion text -> case-insensitive -> substring containment either direction -> else record as unmatched and assign weight = mean of that dimension's criterion weights. Also: if a dimension is entirely missing from the judge's JSON, it is SKIPPED (not zeroed) — but it is also skipped from the S_int sum for BOTH target and reference, so a dropped dimension silently reweights the overall toward the remaining dimensions. Minor operational note for our k=5 harness: if any of the 5 judge calls emits a malformed/partial JSON that loses a dimension, that run's overall is computed on a different weight basis than the others, which would quietly bias the mean. Worth asserting all 4 dimensions are present in each of the k=5 raw outputs before averaging.
